excerpt: A closely parallel tradition from Imam Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq, in which īmān is enumerated as inward belief, verbal confession, and acts of the limbs performed in obedience to the Imam, is preserved in Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān's foundational Fatimid legal compendium.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya — traditions on the meaning of faith

excerpt: The saying that faith is conviction held in the heart, action performed by the limbs, and confession made by the tongue is preserved among the short maxims (ḥikam) ascribed to Imam ʿAlī in the compilation of al-Sharīf al-Raḍī.
source_location_hint: Section of short sayings (Ḥikam / Qiṣār)

excerpt: The structural ordering by which Islam stands to īmān as the outward (ẓāhir) to the inward (bāṭin) — the outward law being the husk whose kernel the daʿwa is instituted to disclose — is articulated as a foundational principle of Fatimid esoteric exegesis.
source_location_hint: On the relation of the outward law to the inward summons

excerpt: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān reports the tradition that faith (īmān) is confession by the tongue, conviction in the heart, and action by the limbs — the tripartite definition that frames the whole apportionment of faith among the bodily organs.
source_authority: Fatimid chief qāḍī under al-Muʿizz li-Dīn Allāh
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, opening discussion on the nature of īmān

excerpt: Nāṣir-i Khusraw expounds the taʾwīl of ritual ablution: the washing of the hands and the limbs prefigures the inward purification of the soul through knowledge received from the duʿāt and the Imām.
source_location_hint: discourses on the inner sense of wuḍūʾ and ṣalāt

excerpt: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān treats the change of qibla from Bayt al-Maqdis to the Kaʿba as an outward test distinguishing those who follow the Messenger from those who turn back, with prayer itself named īmān in the Qurʾān because it is the bodily enactment of the soul's allegiance.
source_authority: Fatimid chief qāḍī under al-Muʿizz li-Dīn Allāh
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Ṣalāt, on the taʾwīl of the qibla

excerpt: On the principle that true faith requires acknowledgement of the rightful Imāms, such that obedience to the Imām of the age is the very condition under which obedience to God and the Messenger is realised.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, opening sections on the obligation of recognising the Imām

excerpt: On the mustajīb at the lowest rank of the daʿwa as one undergoing a second, religious birth and standing in continual need of his ḥadd above for spiritual nurture and gradual ascent.
source_location_hint: discussions on the ranks of the daʿwa and the cultivation of the respondent

excerpt: Threefold classification of believers — the wronger of self, the balanced, and the one who outstrips with good — drawn from the Imāmī taʾwīl of Qurʾān 35:32 and its development in Fatimid jurisprudence and ethics.
source_location_hint: sections on the ranks of the faithful and the ethics of the believer

excerpt: Enumeration of the rights owed to parents, kin, orphans, the poor, and the three categories of neighbours, presented as the taʾwīl of Qurʾān 4:36 within the Fatimid tradition of religious law.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Ṣadaqāt and related chapters on the rights of relatives, neighbours, and the wayfarer

excerpt: Enumeration of the qualities of the perfected believer — meeting all with good, humility regarding one's own deeds, magnifying one's own faults while overlooking the faults of others, patience preferred over status, contentment with little, and unceasing pursuit of knowledge.
source_author: al-Sharīf al-Raḍī (compiler); reported sayings of Imam ʿAlī
source_location_hint: Khuṭba 193 (the Sermon of Hammām on the qualities of the muttaqūn) and related ḥikam

excerpt: This ordering is not a rhetorical adornment but a structural principle of the daʿwa (the summons): the first to respond to the call of God and His Messenger occupy permanent rank above those who answered later, and no later striving can dissolve that order.
source_authority: Imam Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq (via Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān's transmission)
source_location_hint: Vol. 1, Kitāb al-Walāya, traditions on the ranks of the believers and the obligation of precedence in answering the imam's call

excerpt: For faith subsists in the conjunction of belief, utterance, and deed, and cannot exist without all three together. When inward affirmation, spoken word, and bodily act are joined, faith is perfected. But if a person confesses with his tongue and acts in accordance with the law while harbouring in his heart what is opposed to the truth, he is no believer — however much labour he expends in worship, none of his works will profit him.
source_location_hint: Majālis on the inseparability of knowledge ('ilm), profession (qawl), and action ('amal) as the threefold ground of īmān

excerpt: The Paradise of outward Islam is opened by the tongue's confession of lā ilāha illā Allāh, but the Paradise of inner īmān is opened only when that confession is joined to ikhlāṣ, the heart's sincere affirmation.
source_location_hint: sessions treating the levels of faith and the distinction between Islam and īmān

excerpt: al-ʿAql al-Awwal received the knowledge of what was and what shall be only after its acknowledgement of the oneness of Mubdiʿ Taʿālā; thus faith is anterior to knowledge in the very order of origination.
source_location_hint: the discussion of the First Intellect and the procession of the ḥudūd

excerpt: The ascent from ʿilm ẓāhir, the bare awareness that a thing exists, to ʿilm maʿrifa, the realised gnosis that follows inquiry and verification, is the very ladder upon which the degrees of īmān are mounted.
source_location_hint: chapters on īmān and the gradations of knowledge

excerpt: It is reported from Imam Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq that faith is wholly an affair of the heart, while Islam is the outward observance of the rulings of marriage and inheritance, and that whoever enters the circle of Islam has his blood, honour, and property protected.
source_authority: Fatimid daʿwa (Chief Justice under al-Muʿizz li-Dīn Allāh)
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Iman, on the distinction between Islam and īmān

excerpt: The first of the obligations imposed by God upon His servants is the recognition (maʿrifa) of the Imam of the age, after which all other works of religion become acceptable; without it, none is accepted.
source_authority: Fatimid chief qāḍī; foundational legal compendium of the Fatimid daʿwa
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walāya, opening section on the obligation of recognizing the Imam

excerpt: The kalima of tawḥīd has an exoteric face and an esoteric face: the affirmation lā ilāha illā Allāh is its ẓāhir, by which one enters Islam, while lā ilāha illā huwa is its bāṭin, by which one enters the ḥaqīqa of faith through the walāya of the awliyāʾ.
source_authority: Fatimid-era Ismaili philosopher of the daʿwa
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the two registers of the kalimat al-shahāda

excerpt: The First Intellect, brought forth by the Originator (al-Mubdiʿ), bears witness to the divine ipseity by saying lā ilāha illā huwa, and through this witnessing it attains its secondary perfection (kamāl thānī).
source_authority: Fatimid Ismaili philosopher; canonical exposition of the First Intellect
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the tawḥīd of al-ʿAql al-Awwal and its two perfections

excerpt: The believers are arrayed in ranks (darajāt) according to their portion of recognition and obedience; no two believers stand upon a single step, for faith is a ladder whose ascent is graded by knowledge of the ḥudūd.
source_authority: Fatimid Bāb al-Abwāb; chief dāʿī under al-Mustanṣir
source_location_hint: Majālis on the gradations of īmān and the ḥudūd al-dīn

excerpt: The Nāṭiq is the first of the bodily ḥudūd in the world of religion; from him is emanated (yanbaʿith) the Asās, and from the Asās the Imam — and through this chain of inbiʿāth the edifice of religion (haykal al-dīn) is raised in both its Islamic and its īmānī aspects.
source_authority: Ḥujjat al-ʿIrāqayn under al-Hakim; chief metaphysician of the Fatimid daʿwa
source_location_hint: Surūr (chapter) on the bodily ḥudūd and the emanation of the dāʿī hierarchy
